text: Our next paper is actually going to be a video and it will be the superior mesenteric artery syndrome four months following sleeve gastrectomy from Cedars-Sinai Medical Center presented by Dr. Alison Barrett. Good afternoon. Thank you for the privilege to present today. I'm going to be presenting a case report from Cedars-Sinai of a patient who developed superior mesenteric artery syndrome four months following sleeve gastrectomy. There are no disclosures from any of the participants.

The patient was a thirty nine year old female whose status post laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy for a BMI of thirty six. She had a seventy pound weight loss at four months post operatively and developed unrelenting nausea, emesis and epigastric pain. CT scan was performed which demonstrated narrowing of the duodenum between the aorta and the SMA which was consistent with a diagnosis of SMA syndrome. She then underwent mesenteric artery duplex which demonstrated a decreased angle between the aorta and SMA of eighteen degrees as well as a distance between the aorta and SMA of five point eight millimeters, both of which were consistent with the diagnosis. She also underwent an upper GI study which demonstrated a functional obstruction at the third portion of the duodenum.

SMA syndrome is duodenal obstruction between the aorta and the SMA. It's often precipitated by extreme weight loss. Symptoms are fairly nonspecific and can include postprandial fullness, nausea, and epigastric pain. And they're often relieved by positional changes such as sitting forward, lying prone, or in a left lateral decubitus position. The diagnosis of SMA syndrome is by radiologic criteria, including a decreased angle between the aorta and the SMA of six to twenty-two degrees and a decreased distance of two to eight millimeters, both of which our patient had

Surgical options for the treatment of SMA syndrome include gastrojejunostomy, something called Strong's procedure which involves division of the ligament of trites and duodenal mobilization and a duodenal jejunostomy. In this particular patient we opted for a laparoscopic duodenal jejunostomy which is most well described in the literature.

The surgical team begins with an endoscopy, revealing narrowing of the third portion of the duodenum between the aorta and the SMA, with visible vessel pulsations. Laparoscopically, the transverse colon is reflected cranially to identify the middle colic vessels and the SMA's takeoff. The SMA is seen crossing the third portion of the duodenum on the screen's left. Endoscopy confirms the narrowing at the SMA crossing. The duodenum is mobilized, and the transverse mesocolon is incised to expose it. The lateral portion of the second part of the duodenum is opened, and a limb of jejunum is brought up for a lateral side-to-side anastomosis.

The surgeon places a stay suture between the jejunum and duodenum to orient the anastomosis. An enterotomy is made in both the duodenum and jejunum, allowing the passage of a linear stapler. A white load, 45mm stapler is carefully positioned and fired, avoiding compromise of the transverse mesocolon or the SMA at the distal tip. The staple line is inspected, confirmed to be hemostatic and widely patent. The defect is then over-sewn in a hand-sewn two-layer fashion to prevent narrowing of the anastomosis.

The surgeon places a stay suture inferiorly to orient the suture line, followed by the closure of the common defect using two layers of Vicryl suture. The second layer is completed with a lapar tie. The anastomosis is inspected, showing a lateral side-to-side anastomosis. An endoscopy is performed, confirming the anastomosis is wide and hemostatic. The third portion of the duodenum is visible distally, narrowed between pulsatile vessels, with the anastomosis several centimeters proximal on the right.
The surgeon uses an endoscope to perform a leak test by instilling methylene blue into the anastomosis, observing that the irrigation flows distally without any evidence of leakage.

Questions? Let me begin by complimenting you on your use of intraoperative endoscopy. I think it was not only necessary for this case, but it's a very important thing. It took approximately one year for me to get the OR staff to simply go ahead and have flexible endoscope in the room for all four gut cases that were being done laparoscopically, but now it's routine. we had to build that culture. One of the things where you use endoscopy early in your procedure that can occur is you can get excessive distension of the small bowel. Will you talk just a little bit about either your experience with that problem or what techniques you use to prevent that from happening? In this particular case, we had planned to do both intraoperatively, and so we accessed the abdomen laparoscopically and clamped off the distal bowel before instilling any insufflation with the endoscope. And then when we were completed with the first part of the procedure with the endoscope, we took out as much air as we could Do you ever use CO₂ instead of air? Yeah, we do. If we're doing something like this, we try to use CO₂ only. Very good. Thank you very much.
